text:
MELANOMA ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Attending Oncologist: [Attending]
Department: Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine follow-up for metastatic melanoma (Stage IV, M1c). Returning for results of recent PET-CT and discussion of treatment plan. Patient is now approximately 27 months from initiation of immunotherapy (24 months on nivolumab monotherapy maintenance following 3 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab combination).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Renata Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with a history of Stage IV M1c BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma, primary site dorsal trunk (T4b N3c M1c at presentation), who presents today for a landmark follow-up visit in the context of a recently confirmed complete metabolic response on whole-body FDG PET-CT.

To summarize the clinical course briefly: she was initially diagnosed with widely metastatic melanoma involving bilateral axillary and inguinal nodal basins, multiple cutaneous/subcutaneous deposits, and six hepatic metastases. She was treated first-line with dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, achieving a rapid and dramatic partial response with LDH normalization. She experienced a single episode of Grade 2 dabrafenib-induced fever (managed with brief hold and acetaminophen) and two cutaneous SCCs on the left forearm and right shin (excised with clear margins), a class-effect of BRAF inhibition. At roughly 8 months, she progressed with new cervical adenopathy, worsening axillary disease, new hepatic lesions, and a small peritoneal nodule. Resistance profiling via ctDNA (Guardant360) and repeat biopsy of the right cervical node showed acquisition of a MAP2K1 C121S mutation and BRAF amplification, consistent with MAPK reactivation. Targeted therapy was discontinued.

She was then initiated on second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks per the CheckMate-067 schema. After cycle 2 she developed a Grade 1-2 immune-related dermatitis, managed with topical triamcinolone. Following cycle 3, she was hospitalized for 7 days with Grade 3 immune-related colitis (8-10 watery stools/day, sigmoidoscopy-confirmed with crypt abscesses), requiring IV methylprednisolone followed by infliximab 5 mg/kg IV for steroid-refractory disease and an 8-week oral prednisone taper. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued; nivolumab was held and eventually restarted after prednisone was tapered below 10 mg/day. She received 3 of the intended 4 induction cycles total.

Restaging at ~14 weeks from the start of ipi/nivo showed a partial response (~30% reduction). Nivolumab maintenance at 480 mg IV q4 weeks was resumed at approximately 16 weeks from initiation, with subsequent deepening of response on serial imaging. At ~6 months on maintenance, she had achieved a deep partial response with near-disappearance of hepatic metastases and resolution of peritoneal disease. By ~21 months of total immunotherapy, she met criteria for near-complete response, with only a 0.3 cm hepatic hypodensity remaining. At the 24-month mark, after discussion with the patient and consultation with a melanoma center of excellence, a shared decision was made to continue nivolumab for an additional 6 months to pursue complete response, given residual radiographic disease.

Today she returns with results from the PET-CT performed approximately 3 weeks ago, at roughly 27 months from initial ipi/nivo start (23 months on nivolumab maintenance). The scan demonstrated complete metabolic response: no FDG-avid lesions anywhere, and the previously identified residual 0.3 cm hepatic hypodensity was metabolically silent with SUVmax below background liver activity, consistent with fibrotic scar. MRI brain obtained concurrently showed no intracranial metastases.

Ms. Okonkwo reports feeling very well. She has resumed full-time work as a landscape architect, engages in daily outdoor activity, and describes her overall quality of life as excellent. She denies any melanoma-related symptoms. No constitutional symptoms, no pain, no GI complaints. No cutaneous lesions of concern noted by dermatology at her visit approximately 5 weeks ago.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats. Fatigue mild at most, no different from her baseline energy level. Weight stable (she reports a 1-2 lb increase from her last visit, which she attributes to the holiday season).
- HEENT: No visual changes. No oral lesions or mucosal changes. No anterior uveitis flare (last ophthalmology visit approximately 3 months ago, no uveitis activity).
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
- Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or pleuritic pain. No symptoms to suggest immune-related pneumonitis.
- GI: Bowel habits back to baseline — 1-2 formed stools per day. No cramping, hematochezia, tenesmus, or rectal urgency. Oral intake excellent.
- Musculoskeletal: No joint pain, swelling, or myalgias. No proximal muscle weakness.
- Neurologic: No focal neurologic deficits, no headaches.
- Endocrine: Mild cold intolerance persisting from prior hypothyroid state, stable and improved on adjusted levothyroxine. No symptoms of hyperthyroidism (no palpitations, tremor, or heat intolerance).
- Skin: No new pigmented lesions of concern to the patient. Last derm check clear.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria, no edema. Normal urine output.
- Psychiatric: Reports mild anxiety about stopping treatment, which she describes as "knowing intellectually it's the right time, but still emotionally difficult."

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 14, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 63.2 kg. Height 165 cm. BMI 23.2.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Mood pleasant, appropriately anxious about today's discussion.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. PERRLA, EOMI. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical chains bilaterally — previously enlarged right level II node no longer palpable.

Neck: Supple. No palpable lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-enlarged, non-tender.

Axillae: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. The previously enlarged right axillary node is no longer palpable.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender throughout. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No palpable masses. No ascites. Prior incision sites from abdominal wall biopsy are well-healed.

Skin: No new cutaneous melanoma deposits appreciated. Bilateral forearm and shin excision scars from prior SCC resections are well-healed, no evidence of recurrence. No new suspicious pigmented lesions on examination. Scattered atypical nevi on the trunk consistent with her baseline; no clearly concerning changes without comparison to dermatology photographs.

Extremities: No edema. No calf tenderness. No subcutaneous nodules palpable.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.8 k/uL (normal differential), Hgb 13.6 g/dL, Plt 228 k/uL.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cr 0.88 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 94 mg/dL. AST 26 U/L, ALT 29 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L, albumin 4.2 g/dL.
LDH: 131 U/L (NORMAL — within reference range, lowest value recorded throughout course).
TSH: 2.6 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 112 mcg daily).
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal).
Urinalysis: No proteinuria, no hematuria, unremarkable.
CRP: 2.1 mg/L (normal).

All values within normal limits. No laboratory evidence of end-organ immune toxicity.

---

IMAGING RESULTS
PET-CT (whole body FDG) with concurrent MRI brain (gadolinium): No FDG-avid lesions identified anywhere in the body. The previously noted residual 0.3 cm hepatic hypodensity in segment 6 shows no FDG uptake above background liver parenchyma — metabolically quiescent; appearance most consistent with fibrotic/scar tissue. Bilateral axilla: no FDG-avid adenopathy; nodes within normal size limits. No FDG-avid disease in the peritoneum, subcutaneous tissues, soft tissues, or skeleton. MRI brain: no intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal disease. This represents a complete metabolic response after approximately 27 months of immunotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA, BRAF V600E POSITIVE, STAGE IV M1c — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE
This is a landmark visit. Ms. Okonkwo has achieved a complete metabolic response on PET-CT after a complex treatment course that included first-line BRAF/MEK inhibition, second-line combination checkpoint blockade, and prolonged PD-1 maintenance. I spent significant time reviewing this with the patient and her partner today.

Treatment discontinuation plan: We have discussed at length the decision to stop nivolumab today. At this juncture — 27 months from initiation of immunotherapy and 23 months on Opdivo maintenance — she has achieved a complete metabolic response by PET criteria, with the residual hepatic hypodensity appearing consistent with scar. The LDH is at its historical nadir (131 U/L). All measurable disease has resolved. Clinically, she is asymptomatic and ECOG 0.

The rationale for discontinuation is supported by the CheckMate-067 5-year follow-up data and the KEYNOTE-006 long-term data, which show that patients achieving complete responses on PD-1 or CTLA-4/PD-1 therapy with drug discontinuation can maintain durable remission in a meaningful proportion of cases. I discussed with the patient that the estimated risk of relapse following complete response and treatment discontinuation is in the range of 25-35% at 2-3 years in available historical series, and that relapse, if it occurs, is typically manageable with retreatment (either re-challenge with PD-1 therapy or alternative regimens). It was important for me to be transparent that long-term data for patients who stopped after CMR in the M1c setting are still maturing, and that this decision carries genuine uncertainty.

After a thorough discussion and the patient's own reflection following her second opinion at [melanoma center of excellence], she has elected to discontinue nivolumab today. She understands that the next several months will require close surveillance and prompt reporting of new symptoms. Her last nivolumab infusion (480 mg IV) was administered today.

I also spent time addressing her emotional ambivalence around stopping treatment — an entirely appropriate response. I validated that many patients experience this, and we have arranged for a brief session with our oncology social worker prior to her leaving today.

2. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT HISTORY — RESOLVED
- Grade 3 immune-related colitis: fully resolved, off all corticosteroids. GI cleared from follow-up. No residual bowel symptoms. Ipilimumab permanently discontinued; nivolumab now also discontinued.
- Grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism: stable on levothyroxine 112 mcg daily. TSH normal today. Endocrinology to continue long-term follow-up given likely permanent thyroid dysfunction from nivolumab.
- Grade 1-2 immune dermatitis: fully resolved, no recurrence.
- Drug-induced SCC (bilateral, BRAF inhibitor-related): excised, no recurrence on dermatologic follow-up.
- Dabrafenib fever syndrome (Grade 2): fully resolved; dabrafenib/trametinib discontinued at progression over a year ago.

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM (PRE-EXISTING, EXACERBATED BY NIVOLUMAB)
Continue levothyroxine 112 mcg daily. TSH today is 2.6 mIU/L — well-controlled. I counseled the patient that following discontinuation of nivolumab, thyroid function will continue to require monitoring; the immune-mediated component is unlikely to reverse. Recheck TSH in 3 months.

4. SURVEILLANCE PLAN POST-TREATMENT DISCONTINUATION
Given M1c disease with prior hepatic and peritoneal involvement, we are implementing a close surveillance protocol:
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3 months for the first 2 years off treatment, then every 6 months if no recurrence.
- MRI brain with gadolinium every 6 months for the first 2 years (no intracranial disease has been documented throughout but given history of M1c disease, brain surveillance is standard).
- LDH and CMP at each visit — LDH is an important early marker in this patient.
- Dermatology surveillance every 4 months (both for primary melanoma surveillance and ongoing squamoproliferative monitoring, though risk from BRAF inhibitor now historical).
- Endocrinology follow-up for hypothyroidism as above.
- Ophthalmology: continue annual monitoring given history of anterior uveitis; alert ophthalmology to history of immune-related toxicities.
- Patient given written instructions on "red flag" symptoms warranting urgent contact: new palpable mass, severe fatigue, jaundice, GI symptoms, neurologic symptoms, visual change, or any symptom different from baseline.

5. RETURN TO CARE
Next clinic visit in approximately 3 months with updated imaging results. If any concerning symptoms arise in the interim — particularly those that could represent either immune-mediated late toxicities (which can occur weeks to months after cessation of PD-1 therapy) or early recurrence — she has standing instructions to contact the on-call provider or come to the melanoma urgent care clinic.

Total visit time approximately 60 minutes, greater than 50% spent in counseling and coordination of care. The patient left the clinic with her partner, appearing cautiously optimistic and well-informed. This has been a remarkable clinical response in a patient with a high-burden, biologically complex Stage IV melanoma.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist]
Department of Medical Oncology — Melanoma Program
